title: Caloric Surplus Calculator for Muscle Gain | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Ideal Caloric Surplus for Building Muscle
description: Calculate your ideal caloric surplus for muscle gain without excess fat. Based on TDEE and experience level · Free, fast & accurate
intro: Strength training is built on physiological principles (adaptation, supercompensation, specificity). Intensity zones, weekly volume, and recovery are pillars of performance. Calculate your ideal caloric surplus for muscle gain without excess fat. Based on your [TDEE](/tdee-calculator-mifflin-st-jeor) and experience level. Updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you current.
keyTakeaway: **Beginners**: +300–500 kcal surplus. **Intermediate**: +200–400 kcal. **Advanced**: +100–300 kcal. Maximum muscle gain is ~0.5–1 kg/month for beginners and ~0.1–0.25 kg/month for advanced lifters.
useCases: You're starting a bulking phase and want to know how many extra calories to eat.
You want to gain muscle while minimizing fat gain (lean bulk).
You need to recalculate your surplus because your weight changed.
You're comparing aggressive bulk vs lean bulk to choose your strategy.
You're a coach and need to estimate surpluses for your clients.

faq.q: How much caloric surplus do I need to build muscle?
faq.a: It depends on your experience level. **Beginners**: 300–500 kcal extra per day. **Intermediate**: 200–400 kcal. **Advanced**: 100–300 kcal. A larger surplus doesn't accelerate muscle gain—it just adds unnecessary fat.

faq.q: How much muscle can I realistically gain per month?
faq.a: **Beginners**: 0.5–1 kg/month. **Intermediate**: 0.25–0.5 kg/month. **Advanced**: 0.1–0.25 kg/month. These estimates come from Lyle McDonald's research and are widely accepted in sports science.

faq.q: What's the difference between lean bulking and dirty bulking?
faq.a: **Lean bulk**: controlled surplus (+200–400 kcal) with whole foods. **Dirty bulk**: eating whatever you want without tracking. Lean bulking is more efficient because you gain less unnecessary fat.

faq.q: When should I stop bulking and switch to cutting?
faq.a: When your body fat percentage reaches ~15–17% for men or ~25–27% for women. Then do a 4–8 week mini-cut and return to bulking.

faq.q: Do I need supplements to gain muscle?
faq.a: No. The fundamentals are: **caloric surplus + enough protein (1.6–2.2 g/kg) + progressive strength training**. Creatine is the only supplement with strong scientific backing.

faq.q: Does meal frequency matter for muscle growth?
faq.a: **Meal frequency doesn't affect body composition** if total calories and protein are the same. What matters: eating 3–5 meals/day spread out improves protein absorption and satiety.

faq.q: Can I build muscle on a surplus without strength training?
faq.a: No. Without resistance training stimulus, excess calories become **fat, not muscle**. Progressive strength training is essential for calories to be directed toward muscle synthesis.

faq.q: What's the ideal protein intake while bulking?
faq.a: Aim for **1.6–2.2 g per kg of body weight** per day. This ensures adequate amino acids for muscle protein synthesis. More than 2.2 g/kg provides no additional benefits.

explanation:
**Strength training** combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practice. Intensity zones, progressive load, rest days, and nutrition form the complete system.

## How it's calculated

This calculator computes your ideal caloric surplus for muscle gain without excess fat accumulation based on your TDEE and training experience level. It's free and fast.

The calculator applies the formula with your inputs, checking typical ranges and alerting you to any values outside normal parameters.

## Final notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current for 2026 and reviewed periodically.
